skill.name: chatkit-widget
skill.description: Integrates OpenAI ChatKit-style chat widgets into Next.js and React applications, including configuration, layout placement, role-aware display, events, theming, and a backend proxy example.
skill.summary: Add and customize ChatKit chat widgets in Next.js and React apps.
skill.author: Awais68

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-severity patterns, but most weak-crypto, shell-backtick, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by regex use and Markdown examples. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the sample API proxy can expose a server bearer token through an unauthenticated forwarding route, so publication should wait for a safer implementation.

high_findings.title: Secret-Bearing API Proxy Lacks Authentication
high_findings.description: The backend proxy example reads a server-side ChatKit secret and forwards client-supplied endpoint, method, and payload values to the ChatKit API. The sample does not show authentication, role checks, rate limiting, or strict operation mapping before attaching the bearer token, so a deployed copy could let unauthorized users spend or abuse the server credential.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly combines process.env.CHATKIT_SECRET_KEY with request-controlled routing data and an outbound fetch. The risk depends on how users adapt the sample, but the shown route has no visible access control.

medium_findings.title: Remote Widget Script Loaded Without Integrity Controls
medium_findings.description: The client example builds a script URL from configuration and appends it to the page without an integrity check or a strict host allowlist. This is a common widget pattern, but it increases supply-chain risk if the endpoint is changed or compromised.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote script load is explicit and semantically intentional. It may be legitimate for ChatKit integration, but the sample does not include domain pinning or integrity guidance.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Proxy Method Is Too Broad
medium_findings.description: The proxy accepts the HTTP method from the request body and only checks whether the endpoint starts with an allowed prefix. A safer pattern would map named server actions to fixed upstream methods and paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request body is parsed and method defaults from client input before being passed to fetch. The endpoint check exists, but it is broad and not tied to authorization.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm matches in scripts/verify.py are regex parsing and string validation, not hashing, encryption, or signature verification. Similar hits in SKILL.md are Markdown text, colors, JSX paths, or plain examples rather than cryptographic APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited Python lines use regular expressions and lowercase checks only. The inspected Markdown locations do not contain executable weak cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Shell Backtick Alerts Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections occur inside Markdown inline-code spans, tables, and fenced examples. They are documentation formatting, not executed backtick commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and code fences in SKILL.md. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Mostly Legitimate Configuration
low_findings.description: The environment variable detections are primarily documentation for public ChatKit identifiers, a server secret, and API base URL configuration. This is expected for a widget integration skill, although the server secret becomes risky in the proxy pattern described above.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables are named and used consistently with normal configuration. The standalone env access is acceptable, but its use in the proxy route requires hardening.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Validation Output
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detections point to validation and error output rather than host inspection or data collection. No evidence found of commands that enumerate the system, network, users, or files beyond checking SKILL.md and scripts/verify.py existence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Python code validates a supplied skill path and prints errors. The SKILL.md location is an API error response, not local system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Request With Server Credential
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample API route sends Authorization: Bearer CHATKIT_SECRET to an upstream API using route details supplied by a client request. This pattern needs explicit authentication, authorization, allowlisted actions, and rate limiting before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential-bearing fetch is present and directly tied to parsed request body fields. No code in the sample demonstrates compensating access controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Is Partly Confirmed But Not Malicious
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner heuristic combined code examples for network access, credentials, and dynamic loading. This does not prove malicious behavior because the content is instructional Markdown, but the proxy example still contains a real insecure pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous combination exists in sample implementation code, not in hidden executable marketplace code. I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration or prompt injection.

content.value_statement: Teams need a fast way to add branded chat support without rebuilding a chat interface. This skill guides ChatKit widget setup, theming, placement, events, and backend proxy wiring for React and Next.js.
actual_capabilities: Defines a ChatKit configuration structure for project identifiers, theme colors, position, behavior, and copy.
Shows a lazy-loaded React widget component for client-only ChatKit initialization.
Provides Next.js App Router and Pages Router placement examples.
Includes role-based display examples for admins, teachers, students, parents, and guests.
Documents event handling patterns for open, close, message, and error events.
Includes dark mode and branding customization examples.
limitations: The API proxy example needs stronger authentication and authorization before production use.
It does not build a full custom chat interface from scratch.
It assumes a Next.js or React application structure.
It does not cover backend AI model configuration or retrieval setup.

use_cases.title: Add Support Chat to a School Portal
use_cases.description: Place a branded chat widget in a Next.js layout and pass session details to personalize support.

use_cases.title: Control Chat Visibility by Role
use_cases.description: Show different chat access or welcome messages for admins, staff, students, parents, or guests.

use_cases.title: Document Widget Integration Standards
use_cases.description: Create consistent setup guidance for configuration, theming, events, and deployment checks.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Widget Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup in a new app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-widget skill to add a ChatKit widget to my Next.js app. Include the configuration file, component placement, and required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Brand the Widget
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-widget skill to customize the chat widget for our brand. Set theme colors, position, welcome text, and mobile behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Add Role-Based Access
prompt_templates.scenario: Restrict or personalize chat by user role
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-widget skill to show the widget by user role. Include examples for admin, staff, customer, and guest behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Harden the Proxy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-widget skill to review a ChatKit API proxy for production. Add authentication, strict allowed actions, method restrictions, rate limits, and safe error handling.

output_examples.input: Add a chat widget to my App Router layout.
output_examples.output: A concise integration plan with the widget component, layout placement, environment variables, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Make the widget match our school portal.
output: Updated welcome text for students and parents.
Mobile placement guidance for small screens.

output_examples.input: Review this ChatKit proxy before deployment.
output_examples.output: A security checklist covering authentication, fixed upstream actions, secret handling, rate limits, and logging boundaries.

best_practices: Keep secret keys on the server and expose only public identifiers to the browser.
Map client requests to fixed server-side actions instead of forwarding arbitrary methods or paths.
Test widget placement on mobile pages where fixed elements can overlap important controls.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy the proxy example without authentication and authorization checks.
Do not load widget scripts from untrusted or user-controlled domains.
Do not log chat messages or user identifiers unless your privacy policy allows it.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete chat backend?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on widget integration, configuration, and frontend placement. Backend AI behavior is outside its scope.

faq.question: Can it work with both Next.js routing systems?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for App Router layouts and Pages Router app integration.

faq.question: Are secret keys exposed to the browser?
faq.answer: The skill says secrets should stay server-side, but its proxy sample needs stronger access controls before production use.

faq.question: Can I customize the widget appearance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers colors, border radius, position, welcome text, and dark mode adjustments.

faq.question: Does it support role-based chat access?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows role-based display and welcome message patterns for common portal roles.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish as written?
faq.answer: No. The proxy guidance should be hardened before marketplace publication because it can forward requests with a server credential.
